[doctor] it's it's just holding on and i tried taking like medication right i took some ibuprofen and it was fine it just like it keeps coming back and i i read on the label i'm not supposed to take that stuff for more than two weeks right

[doctor] okay
[patient] good patellar patella tracks fine no crepitus alright let's go back to this medial side you're tender right there is that right

[patient] okay i ca n't treat people like you you you you need to work harder i quit complaining okay

[patient] just kinda you're thinking walking the dog
[doctor] yeah rocking kids and walking dogs and going on the occasional swim
[patient] on the occasional swim alright how have you been swimming since you hurt your knee
[doctor] no no i have n't would you recommend that
[patient] well i'm not sure yet it's because we might have to do surgery on your knee here and take that leg off

[patient] look at the x-rays do you or just stop here you can okay okay and so what would what would happen now you would you could pause it
[patient] you're only cup of this
[doctor] let's take a look at your pictures here and alright so this looks pretty just looking at it looks pretty normal looks pretty healthy you can see how i all the edges of the bones here look pretty good i do n't see any bone spurs of that i do n't see anything broken everything looked looks pretty good kneecap's tracking good you can see the space here there's no real space there that's actually cartilage so that looks pretty healthy and you can see the other side looks both both look the same so x-rays look i do n't see anything obvious on that but looking at your knee here you may have hit that tibia bone and kinda pushed that bone back it feels to me like you may very well have sprained your or injured your posterior cruciate ligament that's what it's feeling like
[patient] hmmm
[doctor] i'm not gon na know that for sure or if there is other things going on in there but i think getting an mri scan to look at what you may have done inside there i'm pretty suspicious though that you may have injured that ligament if that's the only thing you did very rarely is this gon na need surgery okay so this may be something we wan na get you into a brace here get you into some therapy and get all your motion back and get your strength back and then maybe check it back in a in a few weeks and see because a lot of times those ligaments can tighten back up
[patient] it's it's odd for just an isolated posterior ligament to need surgery
[doctor] mm-hmm
[patient] but if there's other things going on i ca n't tell you for sure that you did n't injure a meniscus too or maybe chipped some cartilage when that bone shifted so let's let's get an mri scan to look at that if that comes back with just a ligament like i'm suspecting hopefully this is something we can treat with some therapy and some bracing for a while and get this thing back to normal if after that couple months later or so you still have instability and still bothering you then it's possible we may have to do some surgery to fix that ligament
[doctor] okay
[patient] does that make sense
[doctor] yeah yeah so in the meantime though do you want me to still keep taking the ibuprofen yeah i think that's fine for a short if it's helping the big thing right now is we want to get your strength back and your motion back get rid of the swelling that we still see here
[patient] okay
[doctor] so that will help with that ice ice packs a few times a day you can take the ibuprofen up to three times a day and i'll look i'll we'll look at the dose here there are some other medicines we can try that maybe do n't have to take so often that may be easier
[patient] mm-hmm
[doctor] so i can even send in a prescription for you for something that maybe just twice a day a little easier for you to take without skipping doses
[patient] okay
[doctor] okay you can just take it with breakfast and dinner
[patient] mm-hmm
[doctor] we'll try and see if it works better if your ibuprofen works better though go ahead and just go back to that but we can try this other prescription first
[patient] okay
[doctor] it's not very expensive and i can just call it into your pharmacy
[patient] okay
[doctor] and then we can we can get the brace fitted to you today we are not gon na look at a big big custom kind of expensive brace we will just put because it may just be temporary
[patient] mm-hmm mm-hmm
[doctor] and then i will get you a referral to physical therapy something that you can do close to home or close to work
[patient] and then maybe i could see you back here in maybe four to six weeks and recheck this
[doctor] okay how often am i gon na have to go to physical therapy
[patient] i'm gon na kinda leave that up to you and the and the therapist i would start with a couple visits and then we'll just really see how you progress and what you can do at home if what if you have access to some equipment or things that we need to do but i want you to go you know at least a few times so somebody can kinda keep an eye on you make sure you're doing the exercises right
[doctor] okay alright i think that sounds good
[patient] did you get did you select you last night
[doctor] i did
[patient] you yeah what do you think what do you roof for
[doctor] i so i i'm a little heart broken right now i was for the rockets and the way that these nets are crushing them it's just it's just painful

gold_note:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Beverly Howard is a pleasant 33-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right knee pain.

The patient reports that she fell off of a tandem bicycle and struck her right knee on a curb approximately 2 weeks ago. After the injury, she took ibuprofen and treated the wound, but the pain has persisted. She localizes her pain to the medial aspect of the knee. She also reports occasional clicking with full extension of the knee, as well as occasional instability with ambulation. The patient states that her pain is the worst when ambulating up an incline. She denies any previous right knee issues. She enjoys walking her dogs and swimming but has not done this since her injury.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient works in a Children's Hospital.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right knee pain, clicking, and instability.

PHYSICAL EXAM

SKIN: Warm, dry and intact
MSK: 
Examination of the right knee: Tenderness along the medial joint line. Pes bursa is nontender. The epicondyle and posterior aspect of her knee are soft. Popliteal space is nontender. The lateral aspect of her knee is nontender. There is a dime-sized abrasion that is dry and appears to be 1 week old, over the medial patella. No scars. Full extension. Full symmetric flexion. No deformity. Valgus stress of the medial and lateral collateral ligament are intact. Laxity with posterior drawer testing. 1+ posterior drawer test. Negative Lachman test. Patella tracks fine. No crepitus. Negative McMurray test.
Examination of the left knee: Full symmetric flexion. No deformity. Valgus stress of the medial collateral ligament is intact. Lateral collateral ligament is intact.

RESULTS

4 views of the right knee were taken. These reveal no evidence of any fractures or dislocations. Joint space is well preserved. No other abnormalities are noted.

ASSESSMENT

Right knee pain, possible posterior cruciate ligament sprain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, we had a lengthy discussion in regard to her current symptoms. I explained that her x-rays did not reveal any signs of a fracture. I recommend we obtain an MRI of the right knee to evaluate for a possible posterior cruciate ligament injury. In the meantime, I have recommended that the patient be placed in a brace to provide increased support. I have also recommended that the patient attend formal physical therapy to work on her range of motion and strengthening. I advised her to ice her right knee a few times a day to reduce the swelling. She can continue taking her ibuprofen if she finds this helpful, but I am also going to send in a prescription medication she can take less frequently. The patient will follow up with me in 4 to 6 weeks to check on her progress.

ORDERS

MRI, right knee
Prescription medication
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right knee pain following a fall from a tandem bike. He reports that he hit his knee on the curb and took some ibuprofen, which provided temporary relief. However, the pain has persisted and worsened with activities such as walking uphill. He denies any previous knee problems and has been taking ibuprofen as needed.

Past medical history: The patient works as a baby rocker at a children's hospital and enjoys walking his dog. He is physically active and has played sports in high school, but has not been involved in any athletic activities since then. He denies any history of smoking, alcohol, or drug use.

Review of systems: The patient reports pain in the right knee, particularly with weight-bearing activities, but denies any other symptoms.

Current medications: Ibuprofen as needed.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient's knee was tender to palpation on the medial joint line, with a small abrasion on the medial patella. The patient had a slight laxity in the posterior drawer test and a positive one-plus posterior drawer sign. The lateral collateral ligament and medial collateral ligament were intact. The patient had full extension and flexion in both knees, with no deformities or crepitus.

Test Results: X-rays of the knee were reviewed, which showed no obvious fractures or bone spurs, but the doctor suspects a possible injury to the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL).

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a suspected injury to the posterior cruciate ligament of the right knee, likely due to a fall from a tandem bike.

Problem list: Knee pain; suspected posterior cruciate ligament injury; right knee instability; possible meniscus tear or chipped cartilage.

Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 400mg three times a day for pain management. I recommended ice packs a few times a day to reduce swelling. I will send a prescription for a different medication that may be easier to take, which the patient can take twice a day. I will get the patient a referral to physical therapy to improve strength and motion. I will also get a brace fitted to the patient's knee for temporary support. I will re-evaluate the patient in 4-6 weeks to assess progress.

Follow-up:
The patient will follow up in 4-6 weeks to re-evaluate the knee and assess for any need for further treatment, including possible surgery to repair the PCL.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 25-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of knee pain and swelling after a fall from a tandem bike. He reports taking ibuprofen for pain relief but experiencing persistent discomfort. The patient denies any previous knee problems or issues before the accident.

Past medical history: The patient works in a children's hospital as a baby rocker, is active, and swims occasionally. He denies any history of substance abuse, smoking, or significant medical conditions.

Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms related to the knee or other parts of the body.

Current medications: Ibuprofen (dosage not specified) for pain relief.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient's knee is tender on the medial joint line, with a small, 1-week-old abrasion on the medial patella. The knee is stable, with no deformities, and the patient can fully extend and flex the knee. The valgus stress test is negative, and the medial and lateral collateral ligaments are intact. The posterior drawer test is positive with 1+ laxity, and the anterior drawer test is negative. The McMurray test is negative on both sides.

Test Results: X-rays of the knee show no obvious fractures, bone spurs, or other abnormalities.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) injury, possibly with concomitant meniscal or cartilage damage, given the mechanism of injury and physical exam findings.

Problem list: PCL injury; suspected meniscus or cartilage damage; knee pain; knee swelling.

Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen (dosage to be determined) for pain relief, and I will send a prescription for an alternative medication that can be taken twice a day. The patient will use ice packs and will be fitted with a temporary knee brace. I will refer the patient to physical therapy to regain strength and motion in the knee. Follow-up appointment in 4-6 weeks to reassess the knee and progress.

Follow-up: The patient will follow up in 4-6 weeks to reassess the knee and progress.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 30-year-old male who presented with a 2-week history of right knee pain following a fall from a tandem bike. He reports that he hit his knee on the curb and took some ibuprofen, but the pain persists. He has tried taking ibuprofen for more than 2 weeks, which he acknowledges is not recommended. He denies any pre-existing knee problems and states that his knee was normal before the incident.

Past medical history: The patient works in a children's hospital as a baby rocker and spends most of his free time walking his dog. He does not engage in any athletic activities other than the occasional swim.

Review of systems: The patient reports that his knee hurts when walking up hills, but he denies any weakness in the knee.

Current medications: The patient has been taking ibuprofen as needed.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's right knee shows medial joint line tenderness, a dime-sized abrasion over the medial patella, and a little laxity in the posterior drawer. The left knee is normal. The patient has full extension and flexion in both knees, with no deformities or crepitus.

Test Results: The patient had x-rays taken, which showed no obvious abnormalities, but the doctor suspects a possible injury to the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL).

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a PCL injury and possibly other internal knee damage.
Problem list: PCL injury; possible meniscus tear or cartilage damage; knee instability.

Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen up to 3 times a day for pain management, and I will prescribe a different medication that may be easier to take and less frequent. I will also refer the patient to physical therapy and order a brace for temporary support. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in 4-6 weeks to reassess the patient's progress.

Follow-up: The patient will return in 4-6 weeks for a follow-up appointment.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a two-week history of right knee pain following a fall from a tandem bike, which occurred two weeks ago. The patient reports that the knee has been sore since the incident, and she has taken ibuprofen for pain relief. The patient denies any significant knee problems prior to the fall. She works as a baby rocker at a children's hospital and enjoys walking her dog and swimming occasionally. She has not been swimming since the injury.

Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]

Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]

Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen as needed for pain relief.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient has medial joint line tenderness on the right knee, but no tenderness on the lateral side, pes bursa, epicondyle, or popliteal space. There is a small, dry abrasion on the medial patella. The patient has full extension and flexion on both knees, with a slight click in the right knee when bending. The medial collateral ligament is intact, and the lateral collateral ligament is intact. The posterior drawer is positive, with a one-plus grade. The anterior drawer and Lachman tests are negative. The McMurray test is negative on both sides.

Test Results: The patient had X-rays of the right knee, which showed no obvious abnormalities, including no bone spurs or fractures. The kneecap tracks normally, and there is no crepitus.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is likely to have injured her posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) during the fall, which may also involve a meniscus tear or cartilage damage.

Problem list: PCL injury; suspected meniscus tear; cartilage damage; knee pain; knee instability.

Plan:
I recommended the patient get an MRI scan to confirm the suspected PCL injury and to rule out other potential injuries. I advised the patient to continue taking ibuprofen as needed for pain relief, but to try a new prescription medication that may be more convenient to take. I ordered a prescription for the new medication, which can be taken twice a day with breakfast and dinner. I referred the patient to physical therapy to work on regaining strength and motion in the knee. I also ordered a knee brace for the patient to wear temporarily. I will recheck the patient's progress in four to six weeks.

Follow-up: The patient will follow up in four to six weeks to recheck the knee and to assess progress in physical therapy.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presented with a 2-week history of right knee pain after a bicycle accident, where he fell off his tandem bike with his partner. He took ibuprofen and applied a band-aid, but the pain persists. He has not been able to fully straighten or bend his knee without pain. He reports that the pain is worse with walking uphill and that his knee feels unstable.

Past medical history: The patient has no previous knee injuries or conditions. He works as a baby rocker at a children's hospital and goes for occasional swims. He denies any previous knee problems or surgeries.

Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms, but reports that his knee feels unstable.

Current medications: Ibuprofen (as needed) for knee pain.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient's knee was tender to palpation on the medial joint line. The medial joint line tenderness, pes anserine bursa, and epicondyle were nontender. The popliteal space was nontender. There was a small, dry abrasion on the medial patella. The patient had full extension but some pain with flexion. The knee was stable to valgus and varus stress, but there was some laxity in the posterior drawer. The anterior drawer and Lachman tests were negative.

Test Results: X-rays of the knee were reviewed, showing no obvious fractures or bone spurs. The knee cap was tracking normally.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) injury or sprain, possibly with concomitant meniscal or cartilage damage.

Problem list: PCL injury/sprain; possible meniscal or cartilage damage; knee instability.

Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen (600mg) twice a day for pain and inflammation. I recommended ice packs, elevation, and rest. I will refer the patient to physical therapy to improve strength and range of motion. I will also prescribe a temporary knee brace to provide stability. The patient will return in 4-6 weeks for follow-up and reassessment.

Follow-up: The patient will return in 4-6 weeks for follow-up and reassessment.
